content.text: Managed Service Marketplace Offers

Azure Marketplace Managed Service offers are the commercial publication path for Azure Lighthouse delegations. Instead of sending customers a raw ARM template to deploy, an MSP publishes a structured offer through Microsoft Partner Center. Customers discover and subscribe to the offer directly from the Azure portal or Marketplace, and the delegation activates automatically on their subscription or resource group. The Marketplace path is preferred at scale because it provides a governed discovery experience, version management, and Microsoft certification — and removes the operational overhead of distributing and versioning ARM template files to dozens or hundreds of customers.

attrs.alt: Marketplace Managed Service Offer publication lifecycle: Partner Center → Plan Config → Review → Live → Customer Subscribes, plus update loop and public vs private plan comparison

content.text: The ~3 business day Microsoft certification cycle is the key planning constraint. Offer updates — including any change to authorizations or role assignments — require resubmission and re-certification before customers can accept the new version. Plan offer updates accordingly, not as ad-hoc same-day changes.
Offer Structure Terminology
Understanding the Marketplace vocabulary is essential before configuring an offer. The following terms map directly to Partner Center UI sections and the authorizations JSON you will author.
Term
Scope
Key Detail
Offer
Top-level container
One per MSP product (e.g. 'Contoso Managed Azure'). Has a unique publisher ID + offer ID.
Plan
Variant inside an offer
One plan per service tier or customer segment. Each plan has its own authorizations JSON and visibility settings.
Public Plan
Visible to all Azure customers
Discoverable via Marketplace search. Any tenant can subscribe. Role set must be conservative.
Private Plan
Visible to allowlisted tenant IDs
Up to 10 specific customer tenant IDs. Invisible to others. Used for enterprise or custom-scoped engagements.
Authorizations
PrincipalId + roleDefinitionId pairs
Permanent access grants. Same structure as the ARM template registrationDefinitions authorizations array.
Eligible Authorizations
Just-in-time PIM grants
Principal can activate a role for a limited duration. Requires the principal to explicitly activate via PIM portal.
Building the Authorizations JSON

The authorizations JSON is the core of every plan. It specifies which principals in the managing tenant receive which roles on the customer's delegated scope. The structure mirrors the ARM template format you use for direct delegation, so principals, role definition IDs, and PIM configuration are identical.
Role IDs for commonly used built-in roles:
Role Name
Role Definition ID
Contributor
Reader
acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7
Monitoring Contributor
749f88d5-cbae-40b8-bcfc-e573ddc772fa
Monitoring Reader
Owner (FORBIDDEN)
8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635
User Access Administrator (FORBIDDEN)

Owner and User Access Administrator are blocked by Microsoft certification. Including either role definition ID in your authorizations JSON will cause certification failure. Use Contributor plus specific data-plane roles instead.
Creating and Publishing an Offer

The following steps walk through publishing a Managed Service offer end-to-end using the Partner Center UI and the Azure CLI for verification. Partner Center does not yet expose a full REST API for offer creation, so the initial publish is UI-driven; updates can be managed via the Marketplace Partner API.

# --- Pre-publication checklist ---
# 1. Enroll in Microsoft Partner Network (MPN) with Publisher Agreement signed
# 2. Create Commercial Marketplace account in Partner Center
# 3. Collect all MSP principal IDs (user object IDs or group object IDs) from managing tenant
# 4. Determine which roles each principal needs (avoid Owner / UAA)
# 5. Decide: public offer (any customer can subscribe) or private plan (allowlist tenant IDs)

# --- Verify principal IDs in your managing tenant ---
az ad group list --display-name "MSP-Ops-Team" --query "[].{name:displayName, id:id}" -o table
az ad group list --display-name "MSP-Security-Analysts" --query "[].{name:displayName, id:id}" -o table

# --- After offer goes Live, verify customer can see it ---
# Customer side: search for offer by publisher ID in Marketplace
az managedservices offer list --output table

# --- Customer subscribes via CLI ---
az managedservices assignment create \
  --name "contoso-managed-azure-prod" \
  --location "eastus" \
  --managed-by-tenant-id "<MSP-TENANT-ID>" \
  --marketplace-offer-id "<OFFER-ID>" \
  --marketplace-plan-id "<PLAN-ID>" \
  --marketplace-publisher-id "<PUBLISHER-ID>" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<CUSTOMER-SUB-ID>"

# OR customer deploys via Azure portal:
# Home → Marketplace → search offer name → Subscribe → select scope (sub or RG)

# --- MSP verifies delegation is active from managing tenant ---
az managedservices assignment list \
  --query "[?contains(properties.registrationDefinitionId, '<offer-prefix>')]" \
  --output table
Private Plan: Limiting Visibility

Private plans restrict offer discovery to a specific list of customer tenant IDs. This is essential when the authorization set is customer-specific or when the MSP has contractual obligations to prevent other tenants from subscribing to the same plan.

# In Partner Center → your offer → Plans → Plan visibility:
# Set "This plan is private" = Yes
# Add up to 10 tenant IDs in the "Allowed tenants" field

# Tenant IDs to allowlist (customer must provide their Azure AD tenant ID):
CUSTOMER_TENANT_A="11111111-aaaa-bbbb-cccc-222222222222"
CUSTOMER_TENANT_B="33333333-dddd-eeee-ffff-444444444444"

# After adding tenant IDs in Partner Center and republishing:
# Only users in CUSTOMER_TENANT_A and CUSTOMER_TENANT_B will see this plan in Marketplace

# Troubleshoot: customer cannot see plan
# 1. Verify their tenant ID is in the allowlist (exact match, no typo)
# 2. Confirm plan resubmission completed certification (check status in Partner Center)
# 3. Customer must be logged into Azure portal with the correct directory active
# 4. Check: az account show --query tenantId  (must match allowlisted ID)

# Alternatively verify with the Marketplace API from customer side:
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Marketplace/privateStores/default/offers?api-version=2022-09-01" \
  --query "value[?id=='<publisher-id>.<offer-id>']"
Offer Updates and Version Management

Every change to an offer's authorizations — adding a principal, changing a role, adding eligible authorizations — requires resubmission through Partner Center and triggers a new ~3 business day certification cycle. Customers who have already subscribed are NOT automatically updated; they must accept the new offer version from the Service Providers blade in their tenant.

# --- MSP workflow for updating an offer ---

# Step 1: Edit plan in Partner Center
# Navigate to: Commercial Marketplace → Managed Services → Your Offer → Plans → Edit
# Update authorizations JSON (add/remove principals, roles, eligible auth)
# Increment version label (e.g. 1.0.0 → 1.1.0) in plan description

# Step 2: Submit for review
# Partner Center → Review and Publish → Submit
# Wait ~3 business days for certification result (email notification)

# Step 3: After offer goes live, notify customers
# Customers see the update in: Azure portal → All Services → My customers → Service providers

# Step 4: Customer accepts update (customer-side CLI or portal)
az managedservices assignment update \
  --name "<existing-assignment-name>" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<CUSTOMER-SUB-ID>" \
  --marketplace-plan-id "<NEW-PLAN-ID>"

# Step 5: MSP verifies updated delegation is active
az managedservices assignment show \
  --name "<assignment-name>" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<CUSTOMER-SUB-ID>" \
  --query "properties.{status:provisioningState, version:registrationDefinitionId}"

# --- Check for customers still on old plan ---
# From MSP managing tenant using Resource Graph
az graph query -q "
  resources
  | where type == 'microsoft.managedservices/registrationassignments'
  | extend planId = tostring(properties.registrationDefinitionId)
  | where planId contains '<OFFER-ID>'
  | project customerId = subscriptionId, planId, status = properties.provisioningState
  | order by customerId asc
" --query "data" -o table
Bulk Customer Update Notification Script

#!/bin/bash
# bulk-notify-update.sh — enumerate customers on old offer version and print status

OFFER_ID="contoso-managed-azure"
OLD_PLAN_ID="plan-v1"
NEW_PLAN_ID="plan-v2"
MSP_TENANT="<MSP-TENANT-ID>"

echo "=== Customers still on old plan: $OLD_PLAN_ID ==="
az graph query -q "
  resources
  | where type =~ 'microsoft.managedservices/registrationassignments'
  | extend reg = tostring(properties.registrationDefinitionId)
  | where reg contains '$OFFER_ID' and reg contains '$OLD_PLAN_ID'
  | project subscriptionId, reg, status = tostring(properties.provisioningState)
" --query "data[].subscriptionId" -o tsv | while read SUB_ID; do
    echo "Subscription $SUB_ID — pending update to $NEW_PLAN_ID"
    # Optionally send notification via email / Teams webhook
done

echo ""
echo "=== Customers already on new plan: $NEW_PLAN_ID ==="
az graph query -q "
  resources
  | where type =~ 'microsoft.managedservices/registrationassignments'
  | extend reg = tostring(properties.registrationDefinitionId)
  | where reg contains '$OFFER_ID' and reg contains '$NEW_PLAN_ID'
  | project subscriptionId, status = tostring(properties.provisioningState)
" --query "data[].subscriptionId" -o tsv
Marketplace Offer vs Direct ARM Template

Both paths activate the same Lighthouse delegation mechanism. The difference is in discovery, versioning, and the customer onboarding experience. Choosing between them depends on scale, commercial intent, and how much control the MSP needs over the onboarding flow.
Dimension
Marketplace Offer
Direct ARM Template
Discovery
Searchable in Azure Marketplace (public) or private allowlist
MSP must share the template file or URL directly with each customer
Update distribution
Customer notified in portal; accepts new version from Service Providers blade
MSP must resend the updated template and instruct customer to redeploy
Certification
Required: Microsoft reviews authorizations and listing ~3 business days
None; MSP can deploy any roles including Owner (not recommended)
Owner / UAA roles
Blocked by certification
Technically allowed but not recommended; no enforcement
Commercial intent
Supports commercial listing, partner attribution, co-sell eligibility
No commercial catalog presence
Suitable scale
10+ customers, standard service tier, self-service onboarding
1–5 customers, highly customized scopes, rapid iteration
Customer experience
Standard Marketplace subscription flow; no template download needed
Customer must deploy a template; higher friction
Version control
Plan version tracked in Partner Center; customers see current vs new
MSP manages versioning manually; no built-in tracking
Private to specific customers
Private plan with allowlisted tenant IDs (up to 10 per plan)
ARM template shared out-of-band; no Azure-native access control
Certification Requirements and Common Failures

Microsoft runs automated validation plus a manual review on every offer submission. Understanding the automated checks in advance prevents avoidable certification failures. The most common causes of rejection are forbidden roles, missing required listing fields, and authorizations that reference principal IDs from outside the publisher tenant.
Automated Validation Checks
No Owner (8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635) in authorizations — immediate fail
No User Access Administrator (18d7d88d-d35e-4fb5-a5c3-7773c20a72d9) in authorizations — immediate fail
All principalId values must be valid GUIDs resolvable in the publisher's Azure AD tenant
roleDefinitionId must be a recognized Azure built-in role GUID — custom role definition IDs are not accepted
maximumActivationDuration in eligible authorizations must follow ISO 8601 duration format (e.g. PT8H, P1D)
Offer must have at least one published plan with at least one non-empty authorization
Plan description and display name must not be empty or exceed character limits
Checking Certification Status

# Check offer status via Partner Center API
# Requires: Azure AD app with Partner Center API permissions

# Get access token for Partner Center
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token \
  --resource "https://api.partner.microsoft.com" \
  --query accessToken -o tsv)

PUBLISHER_ID="contoso-publisher"
OFFER_ID="contoso-managed-azure"

# List offer submissions and their status
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://api.partner.microsoft.com/v1.0/ingestion/publishers/$PUBLISHER_ID/products/$OFFER_ID/submissions" | \
  python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
for sub in data.get('value', []):
    print(f"Status: {sub.get('targetEnvironment')} — {sub.get('status')}")
    for step in sub.get('workflowDetails', {}).get('steps', []):
        print(f"  {step.get('stepName')}: {step.get('status')}")
"

# Alternative: Check in Partner Center portal
# Commercial Marketplace → your offer → Plan overview → Certification status column
# Green checkmark = passed, red X = failed with reason shown on hover

# Validate authorizations JSON before submission (offline pre-check)
python3 -c "
import json, sys

FORBIDDEN_ROLES = {
    '8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635': 'Owner',
    '18d7d88d-d35e-4fb5-a5c3-7773c20a72d9': 'User Access Administrator'
}

with open('authorizations.json') as f:
    data = json.load(f)

errors = []
for a in data.get('authorizations', []):
    rid = a.get('roleDefinitionId', '')
    if rid in FORBIDDEN_ROLES:
        errors.append(f'FORBIDDEN role {FORBIDDEN_ROLES[rid]} on principal {a.get("principalId")}')
    if len(a.get('principalId', '')) != 36:
        errors.append(f'Invalid GUID format: {a.get("principalId")}')

for e in data.get('eligibleAuthorizations', []):
    dur = e.get('justInTimeAccessPolicy', {}).get('maximumActivationDuration', '')
    if not dur.startswith('P'):
        errors.append(f'Invalid ISO 8601 duration: {dur}')

if errors:
    print('VALIDATION FAILED:')
    for err in errors: print(f'  - {err}')
    sys.exit(1)
else:
    print('Authorizations JSON: OK')
"
Offer Listing Checklist
Publisher display name matches your MPN profile company name
Offer short description (256 chars max) is clear and references Azure Lighthouse delegation
Support URL and privacy policy URL are reachable public URLs
Contact information (support email, engineering contact) is filled
At least one screenshot (1280x720px) uploaded showing the service offer or architecture
All plans have unique plan IDs and display names
No plan references a role with Owner or User Access Administrator
Eligible authorization durations are in ISO 8601 format (PT8H, P1D, etc.)
Private plan allowlist (if used) contains valid Azure AD tenant GUIDs, not subscription IDs
Managing Multiple Plans for Customer Tiers

A single Marketplace offer can contain multiple plans for different service tiers — for example, a Foundation plan with Reader access, a Standard plan adding Monitoring Contributor and Contributor, and a Premium plan adding eligible Owner elevation via PIM. Each plan has its own authorizations JSON and visibility settings, published under the same offer ID.

# Example: three-tier offer structure
# Offer: contoso-managed-azure
#   Plan: foundation   (public,  Reader only, no eligible auth)
#   Plan: standard     (public,  Contributor + Monitoring Contributor)
#   Plan: premium      (private, Contributor + eligible Owner via PIM, allowlisted tenants)

# Customer subscribes to a specific plan:
az managedservices assignment create \
  --name "contoso-managed-azure-standard" \
  --location "eastus" \
  --managed-by-tenant-id "<MSP-TENANT-ID>" \
  --marketplace-offer-id "contoso-managed-azure" \
  --marketplace-plan-id "standard" \
  --marketplace-publisher-id "contoso-publisher" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<CUSTOMER-SUB-ID>"

# Upgrade a customer from standard to premium (requires plan update in Partner Center first)
az managedservices assignment update \
  --name "contoso-managed-azure-standard" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<CUSTOMER-SUB-ID>" \
  --marketplace-plan-id "premium"

# List all customer assignments grouped by plan
az graph query -q "
  resources
  | where type =~ 'microsoft.managedservices/registrationassignments'
  | extend regId = tostring(properties.registrationDefinitionId)
  | where regId contains 'contoso-managed-azure'
  | extend planTier = case(
      regId contains 'premium', 'premium',
      regId contains 'standard', 'standard',
      regId contains 'foundation', 'foundation',
      'unknown')
  | summarize customerCount = count() by planTier
  | order by customerCount desc
" --query "data" -o table
Summary

Marketplace Managed Service offers are the production-grade path for MSPs distributing Azure Lighthouse delegations at commercial scale. Publishing through Partner Center provides customer discoverability, Microsoft-certified authorization sets, built-in version management, and a governed subscription flow. The key operational constraints are: the ~3 business day certification cycle on every authorization change; the absolute block on Owner and User Access Administrator roles; and the requirement that customers actively accept offer updates rather than receiving them automatically. Private plans handle enterprise customers who need restricted visibility and custom role sets. Once an offer is live, automation — Resource Graph queries to track which customers are on which plan version, CLI scripts to assist customer updates, and validation scripts to pre-check authorizations JSON before submission — reduces the manual overhead of managing a large, versioned MSP catalog.
